questions.text: At the transition height, what signature combination is required for a user transaction to be valid?

options.text: Only an ECDSA signature
Both an ECDSA signature and a post-quantum signature
Only a post-quantum signature (ECDSA is immediately dropped)
A one-time migration proof instead of any signature

questions.explanation: MST’s hybrid transition requires both signatures to maintain backward compatibility. Option C is incorrect because ECDSA is dropped only later (Hₜ + 6 months). Option A describes pre-transition behavior.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

MST’s hybrid transition requires both signatures to maintain backward compatibility. Option C is incorrect because ECDSA is dropped only later (Hₜ + 6 months). Option A describes pre-transition behavior.

questions.text: How does a validator node know which post-quantum key is associated with a user’s address?

options.text: It is embedded in the transaction itself
It queries an on-chain key registry contract
It derives the PQ key from the ECDSA public key using a hash
The user sends a separate registration message before each transaction

questions.explanation: The PQKeyRegistry contract stores the mapping. Validators call getPQKey(user) during verification. This avoids embedding keys in every transaction (option A) and does not require repeated registration.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

The PQKeyRegistry contract stores the mapping. Validators call getPQKey(user) during verification. This avoids embedding keys in every transaction (option A) and does not require repeated registration.

questions.text: What happens to legacy wallets that do not produce a post-quantum signature after the transition height?

options.text: They are automatically upgraded by the network
Their transactions are rejected by validators
They can still send transactions, but with a warning
The network forks to keep them operational

questions.explanation: After Hₜ, validators enforce the hybrid requirement. Non-upgraded wallets cannot transact, but their funds can be recovered via the force-migrate relay.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

After Hₜ, validators enforce the hybrid requirement. Non-upgraded wallets cannot transact, but their funds can be recovered via the force-migrate relay.

questions.text: Why does MST use Falcon for validator block signatures instead of Dilithium?

options.text: Falcon is quantum-resistant while Dilithium is not
Falcon produces smaller signatures, reducing block propagation time
Dilithium is not suitable for consensus algorithms
Falcon is the only NIST-approved signature scheme

questions.explanation: Falcon signatures are ~700 bytes vs Dilithium’s ~2.5 KB, which is critical for high-frequency block signing. Both are quantum-resistant and NIST-approved.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

Falcon signatures are ~700 bytes vs Dilithium’s ~2.5 KB, which is critical for high-frequency block signing. Both are quantum-resistant and NIST-approved.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the “shadow-signing” phase for validators?

options.text: To test the economic incentives of slashing
To allow validators to practice signing blocks with Falcon without enforcement
To mine extra blocks in parallel to the main chain
To generate secret keys that are later revealed

questions.explanation: During shadow-signing, validators optionally append Falcon signatures. Other nodes verify them, allowing performance testing and bug detection under live conditions before enforcement.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

During shadow-signing, validators optionally append Falcon signatures. Other nodes verify them, allowing performance testing and bug detection under live conditions before enforcement.

questions.text: How does the force-migrate relay contract know which assets to move for an unregistered user?

options.text: It scans the blockchain for the user’s balance
The user’s SARAL wallet pre-authorises a migration allowance during onboarding
The user must manually initiate migration from a backup device
Any third party can call it, but only for assets locked in DeFi

questions.explanation: During wallet creation, a small proxy contract is deployed that pre-approves the relay contract to migrate assets to a quantum-safe address derived from the user’s Dilithium key. No user action is needed at migration time.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

During wallet creation, a small proxy contract is deployed that pre-approves the relay contract to migrate assets to a quantum-safe address derived from the user’s Dilithium key. No user action is needed at migration time.

questions.text: Which of the following best describes the governance role in MST’s quantum migration?

options.text: Governance has no role; the transition is hard-coded
Governance votes to set the transition height and can accelerate it if needed
Governance selects which users must migrate first
Governance manually verifies each quantum-safe key

questions.explanation: The on-chain DAO sets the block height for Hₜ. This allows the community to react to quantum breakthroughs by accelerating the timeline, while maintaining a predictable default.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

The on-chain DAO sets the block height for Hₜ. This allows the community to react to quantum breakthroughs by accelerating the timeline, while maintaining a predictable default.

questions.text: If a user loses access to their SARAL wallet after the transition height but before registering a PQ key, how can their funds be recovered?

options.text: Through the standard SARAL biometric recovery flow, which regenerates the Dilithium key from the enclave backup
The funds are permanently lost
The user must contact MST support to manually override the registry
The force-migrate relay requires a court order to act

questions.explanation: SARAL’s recovery uses Shamir secret sharing of the enclave seed. The Dilithium keypair is derived from the same seed as the ECDSA keypair. Thus, standard SARAL recovery restores both keys, and the user can immediately sign hybrid transactions. No external override is needed.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: A

SARAL’s recovery uses Shamir secret sharing of the enclave seed. The Dilithium keypair is derived from the same seed as the ECDSA keypair. Thus, standard SARAL recovery restores both keys, and the user can immediately sign hybrid transactions. No external override is needed.

questions.text: "MST requires a hard fork to enforce post-quantum signatures on transactions."

📊 Marks Breakdown

1 mark: States FALSE.
+1 mark: Explains that hybrid transactions are a subset of old rules, avoiding a permanent fork.

questions.text: Explain, in detail, how a quantum computer would attack an ECDSA-secured wallet, and describe the exact sequence of events that MST’s quantum-ready pillar uses to protect that wallet before, during, and after the quantum break. Include the roles of the key registry, the hybrid transaction, and the force-migrate relay.
questions.explanation: 1 mark: Correctly describes the quantum attack (Shor’s algorithm recovers private key from public key).1 mark: Mentions SARAL dual-key generation and on-chain registration of Dilithium pubkey.1 mark: Explains hybrid signature enforcement at Hₜ and why it defeats the attack.1 mark: Describes post-transition single-sig safety.1 mark: Includes the force-migrate relay as a safety net for non-upgraded users.
questions.modelAnswer:
📊 Marks Breakdown

1 mark: Correctly describes the quantum attack (Shor’s algorithm recovers private key from public key).
1 mark: Mentions SARAL dual-key generation and on-chain registration of Dilithium pubkey.
1 mark: Explains hybrid signature enforcement at Hₜ and why it defeats the attack.
1 mark: Describes post-transition single-sig safety.
1 mark: Includes the force-migrate relay as a safety net for non-upgraded users.

questions.text: You are the CTO of a DeFi protocol deploying on MST. Design a smart-contract modifier that ensures only quantum-safe users can interact with your vault after the transition height. Describe the modifier’s logic, the on-chain call it must make, and explain why this pattern protects your protocol from both classical and quantum attackers.
questions.explanation: 1 mark: Modifier logic correct: checks pqRegistry.getPQKey(msg.sender).length > 0.1 mark: Queries the correct registry contract (not a local mapping).1 mark: Explains that without the PQ key, the transaction cannot have a valid hybrid signature.1 mark: Optional block-height gating (check block.number >= transitionHeight).1 mark: Addresses unregistered users (revert with helpful message or suggest force-migrate).
questions.modelAnswer:
📊 Marks Breakdown

1 mark: Modifier logic correct: checks pqRegistry.getPQKey(msg.sender).length > 0.
1 mark: Queries the correct registry contract (not a local mapping).
1 mark: Explains that without the PQ key, the transaction cannot have a valid hybrid signature.
1 mark: Optional block-height gating (check block.number >= transitionHeight).
1 mark: Addresses unregistered users (revert with helpful message or suggest force-migrate).
